title: Running GPT-5, GLM-5.1, and Claude 4 in Production: What Actually Works
description: Three months of real-world usage across my AI platform, memory engine, and desktop app. Not benchmarks — actual task performance, failure modes, and what I'd choose differently.
author: Kevin

content.body: Everyone publishes benchmark comparisons. Almost nobody publishes what happens when you actually run these models in production for three months. I've been running GPT-5, GLM-5.1 FP8, and Claude 4 across my AI Business Platform, KCAI Desktop, and Brain-Forge memory engine. Here's what I've actually seen — the good, the bad, and the expensive.

content.heading: Coding: Where Each Model Wins and Fails
content.body: I throw a lot of multi-file refactoring at these models. GPT-5 handles context across 50+ files without dropping threads — its 256K window is real, not marketing. GLM-5.1's FP8 variant runs about 2x faster, which matters more than you'd think when you're iterating on agent workflows. Claude 4 is the one I trust for security review — it catches injection vectors the others miss, but it's slower and sometimes refuses tasks that are fine.

content.heading: Agentic Tool Use: The Real Bottleneck
content.body: This is where benchmarks completely mislead you. Function calling benchmarks test single calls. In production, you're chaining 15-20 tool calls across multiple agents. That's where reliability differences become stark. GLM-5.1's native function calling is the most consistent for multi-step agent workflows — it rarely hallucinates tool parameters. GPT-5 sometimes overthinks simple calls, adding unnecessary steps. Claude 4 is the safest but slowest, which kills throughput when you're running parallel agent swarms.

callout.text: For agent orchestration with 15+ agents, GLM-5.1 FP8 gives the best speed-to-reliability ratio. GPT-5 for complex planning. Claude 4 for anything where a bad tool call costs real money.

content.heading: The Cost Reality
content.body: Running these in production adds up fast. Here's my actual monthly spend, no rounding:
list: **GPT-5**: ~$200/month — 50K requests, mostly agent dispatch and complex reasoning
**GLM-5.1 FP8**: ~$80/month — 100K requests, high-volume tasks where speed and cost matter
**Claude 4 Sonnet**: ~$120/month — 30K requests, code review and safety checks
**Local (llama.cpp)**: ~$15/month electricity — unlimited inference, privacy-sensitive work, offline dev

content.heading: Failure Modes Nobody Talks About
content.body: Benchmarks don't show you how models break in production. Here's what I've hit:
list: **GPT-5 context drift** — at 200K+ tokens, it starts referencing earlier context that's been superseded by newer instructions. Not a hallucination, more like losing track of what's current.
**GLM-5.1 edge cases** — handles 95% of cases cleanly but occasionally produces syntactically valid but semantically wrong code on unusual patterns. Caught by tests, not by the model.
**Claude 4 refusal creep** — over a long session, it becomes increasingly cautious, refusing tasks it handled fine earlier. Resetting the conversation fixes it.
**All three** — streaming SSE responses occasionally drop the last 1-3 tokens. Always buffer and verify completion.

content.heading: What I'd Choose Differently
content.body: If I started over, I'd front-load more work to GLM-5.1 FP8 and use GPT-5 only for the hard 20% of tasks. The cost difference is significant and the quality gap on routine work is smaller than benchmarks suggest. Claude 4 I'd keep exactly where it is — security review only, where the cost is justified by the risk it prevents.
